variables.content:
At each density, spin polarization, and finite system size the discrete DMC band values \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) (where \\(k=|\\mathbf{k}|\\)) were fitted over an extended wavevector interval to a quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4},
      $$
      with fitted coefficients \\(\\alpha_{0},\\alpha_{2},\\alpha_{4}\\). The quasiparticle effective mass was then obtained from the definition
      $$
      m^{*}=\\frac{k_{F}}{\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}},
      $$
      where \\(k_{F}\\) is the Fermi wavevector of the infinite 2D HEG at the given density and spin polarization and the derivative is evaluated at \\(k=k_{F}\\). It was observed that extracting the derivative from fits restricted to a very narrow interval immediately surrounding \\(k_{F}\\) yields ill-behaved (pathological) estimates of \\(m^{*}\\), whereas performing the quartic fit over a larger modest wavevector range around \\(k_{F}\\) produces stable, systematically varying estimates of \\(m^{*}\\). Averaging the band behaviour over such a modest \\(k\\)-range therefore provides a controlled practical procedure to suppress the finite-cell and Hartree–Fock-like near-\\(k_{F}\\) fluctuations that cause the pathological behaviour.

Assume the true thermodynamic-limit single-quasiparticle band of a 2D HEG is a smooth function \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) in a neighbourhood of the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: fitting the finite-cell discrete DMC band values to the quartic polynomial model
      $$
      \\mathcal{E}(k)=\\alpha_{0}+\\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\\alpha_{4}k^{4}
      $$
      over a modest interval \\([k_{F}-\\Delta k,\\,k_{F}+\\Delta k]\\) (with \\(\\Delta k\\) chosen so that the interval is not extremely small and contains sufficient discrete \\(k\\)-points to characterise curvature) yields an estimate of the derivative \\(\\left.\\dfrac{d\\mathcal{E}}{dk}\\right|_{k_{F}}\\) that is an adequately accurate approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative for the purpose of computing the quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}=k_{F}/(d\\mathcal{E}/dk)_{k_{F}}\\), provided the quartic form captures the local curvature of \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\).

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

For a single-particle energy band E(k) of a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, with scalar wave number k=|𝗸|, fitting discrete DMC-computed band values E(k_i) within the finite interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk] to the quartic polynomial E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4 and then evaluating the derivative at k_F from the fit as (dE/dk)_{k_F}=2α_2 k_F+4α_4 k_F^3 provides an analytic approximation to the true thermodynamic-limit derivative at k_F suitable for estimating the quasiparticle effective mass m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}, provided the chosen Δk is neither so small that pathological near-k_F fluctuations dominate nor so large that physics beyond quartic band curvature is sampled.

The fitted DMC bands deviate from a purely quadratic dependence on $k$: when the fitted quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ is used, the quartic coefficient $\alpha_4$ is positive for the paramagnetic HEG at $r_s=5$ and $10$ a.u., while $\alpha_4$ is negative for the ferromagnetic HEG at all densities studied; because these fitted bands are non-quadratic, thermodynamic properties that depend on integrals over the dispersion (for example temperature-dependent contributions to the free energy and specific heat at elevated temperatures) are predicted to depart from the free-particle-like behavior that would follow from a strictly quadratic dispersion $\propto k^2$.

Let the band energy near Γ be sampled over the axial wavevector interval k∈[−0.1,0.1] (units of 2π/a where a is the axial lattice constant) and fitted to a quadratic function ε(k)=C_{1}k^{2}+C_{2}k+C_{3}; the proposition is that performing this quadratic fit over the specified k window yields an accurate and numerically stable estimate of the curvature coefficient C_{1} such that the effective mass m^{*}=ħ^{2}/(2C_{1}) is insensitive, within the reported significant figures, to modest changes in the chosen fit window and to the k-point sampling density used in the calculations.

To obtain the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass from finite-N DMC band data one may (i) fit the computed isotropic band values E(k), with k = |𝗸|, in a finite interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk] about the Fermi wave vector k_F to the quartic polynomial E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4 (with fit coefficients α_0, α_2, α_4), (ii) compute the band derivative at k_F from the fit as (dE/dk)_{k_F}=2α_2 k_F+4α_4 k_F^3 and define the quasiparticle effective mass by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}, and (iii) evaluate m^*(N) for several simulation-cell electron counts N and fit the N dependence to the single power-law model m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} (with fit parameters m^*(∞), b, and γ) to obtain the thermodynamic-limit mass m^*(∞); when the quartic fit in step (i) is performed over a sufficiently wide Δk about k_F (so as not to be dominated by pathological near-k_F fluctuations) this three-step procedure yields stable extrapolated values m^*(∞).

Fitting a quadratic dispersion relation E(k) = E0 + (ħ²k²)/(2m*) (where E is energy, E0 is a reference energy, ħ is the reduced Planck constant, k is the wavevector magnitude measured along a chosen crystallographic path, and m* is the fitted effective mass) to DFT-computed band energies over a finite selected momentum-space path segment that traverses a predominantly flat region of the band produces a fitted effective mass m* (for example the value m*Γ-K = 74.12 m_e reported for a fit along the Γ–K direction), and this fitted m* is a meaningful descriptive measure of carrier inertia and tendency toward localization along that direction even when the underlying band is intrinsically non-parabolic, while recognizing that the numerical value is sensitive to the chosen fitting window and to deviations of the band from a true quadratic form.

Extracting conduction-band effective masses $m^{*}$ at the X point (reported in units of the free-electron mass $m_{0}$) by fitting a quadratic function $E(k)\approx E_{0}+\frac{\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m^{*}}$ to computed conduction-band energies along the longitudinal ($\Delta$, $\Gamma$–X) and transverse ($Z$, X–W) directions is a valid procedure provided the fitting region is sufficiently close to the conduction-band minimum so that higher-order (nonparabolic) terms are negligible over the $k$-space range used; under these conditions the quadratic fit yields effective masses accurate to roughly $\sim10\%$.

Given a discrete set of DMC-computed occupied-band samples $\{\mathcal{E}_i\}$ at wave-vector magnitudes $\{k_i\}$ with $0\le k_i\le k_F$, the proposition is that performing a least-squares fit of a smooth quartic function $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_0+\alpha_2 k^2+\alpha_4 k^4$ over the full occupied range produces an estimate of the derivative $\partial\mathcal{E}/\partial k$ at $k=k_F$ that is substantially less sensitive to local numerical noise, Hartree-Fock-like residual pathological fluctuations near $k_F$, and finite-size irregularities than a numerical derivative obtained by differencing only a few near-$k_F$ points, so that the quartic-fit derivative is a reliable estimator of the quasiparticle-band slope at $k_F$ for the data sets and system sizes studied.
